tier_justification: Single work-unit PR, no irreversible/outward surface, but does NOT qualify for tier-0 exemption: the goal state's own coverage (RailRight.test.tsx's 5 tests) is written by this same task, so it shares an oracle with the implementation (anti-gaming rule 4) and cannot itself satisfy the 'existing test already covers the goal state' predicate. Falls through to tier 1: independent end-state evals derived from the plan/spec's stated interfaces, not from the implementer's own test file.

evals.assert: Full dashboard app test suite (all files, not just the new one) is green on a checkout of the PR head SHA — proves no regression in the other 26 pre-existing test files, not just the 5 new tests.
evals.negative_control: Introduce a syntax error in src/components/RailRight.tsx (e.g. an unclosed brace) on a scratch copy and confirm `npm test` fails/errors rather than reporting green.
evals.evidence: npm test on rebased head 61ed01986ab548d0a446b0fd827eabda7471ca18: Test Files 28 passed (28), Tests 418 passed (418). Baseline pre-change was 27 files/413 tests, so this is +1 file/+5 tests, no regressions.

evals.assert: ButtonUiState (RailRight.tsx) actually gained a lastOutcome: "completed" | "failed" | null field wired into EMPTY_UI_STATE — checked via source grep against the merged file, independent of whatever the implementer's own test asserts.
evals.negative_control: grep for a field name that does not exist in the file (e.g. 'lastOutcomeXYZ') and confirm the grep count is 0, proving the grep genuinely discriminates presence vs absence.
evals.evidence: grep -c 'lastOutcome' returned 8 (interface field, EMPTY_UI_STATE, derivation, className usage, comments). Negative control grep -c 'lastOutcomeXYZ' returned 0.

evals.assert: hud.css defines both hud-complete-flash and hud-fail-flash keyframes AND .hud-cmd.completed / .hud-cmd.failed selectors targeting .hud-bullet, matching the spec's exact treatment (not a differently-named or differently-scoped equivalent).
evals.cmd: cd skills/dashboard/app && grep -c '@keyframes hud-complete-flash' src/styles/hud.css && grep -c '@keyframes hud-fail-flash' src/styles/hud.css && grep -c '.hud-cmd.completed .hud-bullet' src/styles/hud.css && grep -c '.hud-cmd.failed .hud-bullet' src/styles/hud.css
evals.negative_control: grep for a keyframe name with a typo (e.g. '@keyframes hud-compete-flash') and confirm 0 matches, proving the check discriminates the exact required name from a near-miss.
evals.evidence: All 4 greps returned count 1 each (both keyframes and both .hud-cmd.completed/.failed .hud-bullet selectors present exactly once). Negative control grep -c '@keyframes hud-compete-flash' returned 0.

evals.assert: The button's className expression in RailRight.tsx includes ui.lastOutcome in its template, so the completed/failed class is actually wired onto the rendered <button>, not just present as dead state.
evals.cmd: cd skills/dashboard/app && grep -n 'className=' src/components/RailRight.tsx | grep 'hud-cmd' | grep -c 'lastOutcome'
evals.negative_control: grep the same className line for a field guaranteed absent from it (e.g. 'inputValue', which belongs to a different part of ButtonUiState) and confirm 0 matches — proves the positive check isn't a vacuous always-true grep.
evals.evidence: grep chain returned 1 (className line contains ui.lastOutcome). Negative control grepping the same line for 'inputValue' returned 0.

evals.assert: RailRight.test.tsx's 5 required tests (completed class, failed class, auto-clear after timeout, no cross-contamination between a button's stale timer and its next run, cleanup-on-unmount) are each present as a distinct test case addressing that exact scenario, not merged/skipped/renamed away from the plan's specification.
evals.verifier_instruction: Read skills/dashboard/app/src/components/RailRight.test.tsx at the given PR's head SHA. For each of the 5 scenarios listed in this eval's assert field, confirm there is a distinct it(...)/test(...) block whose test body actually exercises that scenario (not just a title matching the words). Report each of the 5 as present/absent with a one-line reason, and an overall pass/fail (pass requires all 5 present and non-trivial -- no test that only asserts true or lacks any assertion). You are NOT given the implementation conversation -- grade only from the file content and this instruction.
evals.evidence: Fresh general-purpose subagent (no implementation-conversation context), graded cold from file content: all 5 scenarios present as distinct it() blocks with real feature-breaking assertions. Independently ran `npx vitest run src/components/RailRight.test.tsx`: 5 passed (5). Overall verdict: PASS.
